title: Design Patterns 101
scan: What you will learn: the four patterns in every DEPT® project — Observer, Dispatcher, Strategy, Producer/Consumer — and the one-line reason to reach for each
Why it matters: pattern vocabulary is the shared language between architect and engineer — without it, code review is description, not analysis
Patterns 101 is a navigation guide, not a tutorial — each pattern has a dedicated section deeper in the course
If a PR description cannot name the pattern it implements, the implementation is probably wrong

blocks.html: attern is a named, reusable solution to a problem that keeps coming back. You don't reinvent structure every time you hit it — you recognise the shape and reach for the known answer. Four show up constantly in the pages that follow:

cards.title: Pub / Sub
cards.body: Something happens; whoever cares is notified. Powers events, listeners & the dispatcher.

cards.title: Central Switch
cards.body: One place receives every signal and routes it by type. Decouples senders from receivers.

cards.title: Swap Behaviour
cards.body: Sort, filter, format — pluggable rules you pass in rather than hard-code.

cards.title: Queue
cards.body: One side puts work down, another picks it up later. The basis of jobs & queues.

blocks.html: Patterns aren't rules to obey — they're a shared vocabulary so two engineers mean the same thing by "dispatcher."

blocks.source:
Synchronous          Asynchronous
              ┌──────────────────┬──────────────────┐
Single-       │                  │                  │
consumer      │    Strategy      │    Observer      │
              │                  │                  │
              ├──────────────────┼──────────────────┤
Multi-        │                  │                  │
consumer      │   Dispatcher     │ Producer/Consumer│
              │                  │                  │
              └──────────────────┴──────────────────┘

blocks.html: DEPT® field pattern — add a "Pattern" field to PR templates. Engineers who cannot fill it in are prompted to think structurally before the reviewer does. Takes 30 seconds to add; saves 20 minutes of review discussion per PR.
